📺YouTube Summarizer
ホーム
Unknown

Untitled

▶ YouTubeで視聴

シリコンバレー創業者のためのエチケット：信頼を構築する「低心拍」の技術

Sam Lessin（Slow Venturesパートナー、元Facebook製品担当副社長、2度の創業者）が、2026年の創業者にとってなぜエチケットが重要なのかを解説。技術が社会の中心となり、信頼の構築が困難になる中、適切なエチケットは必須スキルとなっている。Lessinのエチケット本と講座は、創業者に「低い心拍で部屋に現れる」方法と迅速な信頼構築を教え、驚くほど人気を博している。

エチケットが重要な理由

エチケットとは「低い心拍で部屋に現れる」ためのスキルである。Kleiner Perkinsのホリデーパーティーで世界中のベンチャーキャピタリストとCEOが集まる場面を想像する。最初の会社で若手創業者として参加し、「これは私の唯一のチャンスだ」と思うと、非常に取引的になる。エネルギッシュすぎる姿勢で現れると、相手を怖がらせ、完全に間違った雰囲気を投影してしまう。これは唯一のチャンスではない。他の機会もある。豊かさの自信と落ち着きを持って現れるべきである。

**99.9%**のスタートアップにとって、製品だけで成功することは稀である。エチケットを理解することは、非常にうまくいくこととビジネスチャンスを逃すことの違いになる可能性がある。心拍が高すぎて現れ、小さな関係の機会を燃やしてしまうなら、なぜこのスキルを持ちたくないのか。技術が単なる可愛い余興ではなく、主要な取引となり、人々が仕事を失うことを心配している時代、人々がどこにいるかを理解し、信頼を構築し、期待される行動を鏡写しにする能力は重要なツールである。

導入と部屋に入る

早めに到着する。半時間早すぎるのは少し奇妙だが、バッファー時間を確保すること。5分遅れて部屋に入ってくると心拍が上がる。待合室で一拍置き、相手を待たせる動きを育てるのが望ましい。遅れたら単純に謝り、先に進む。5つの火災警報のようにする必要はない。

握手は強く、相手の手を潰さない。名前を繰り返すことは非常に価値がある。「Lenny、お会いできて嬉しい」と言うことで、相手の名前を覚えようとしていることを示す。多くの人が多くの人に会う。「お会いできて嬉しい」と言って先に進むと、相手の名前を覚えるのが難しくなる。また、相手を単なる数字や潜在的なチェックではなく、接続しようとしていることを示す。

相手が遅れた場合、気まずくさせない。VCとして30分ごとにバックツーバックでスケジュールされていることが多い。遅れることは起こる。創業者の多くは、VCが遅れたら謝罪し、事前にメールを送ることを知っている。しかし、時々創業者が遅刻に対して非常に憤慨する。数分遅れたことが取引の決定的な要因であるなら、次の25分の会議時間を浪費していることになる。

アイコンタクトは非常に重要である。コンピュータ画面の前にいて、6つの異なるウィンドウを同時に見ることに慣れている時代に、人々は会うために時間を取っている。会話に参加していることを示すための敬意の問題である。神経多様性の人にとっては難しいが、努力することは重要である。努力のシグナル自体が、実際の行動と同じくらい重要である。

パートナーを紹介する際、パートナーを最初に紹介する。名前を忘れることがよくある。エチケットでは「Lenny、妻のJessicaを紹介させて」と言う。名前を覚えていない場合、「Jessica、あなたを紹介したい」と言い、少し宙に浮かせると、Lennyが手を差し出して「Jessica、お会いできて嬉しい」と言い、名前を再確認できる。

会話と関係構築

会話に人々を歓迎する。特に権力動態が奇妙な場面で、有名なVCや創業者が部屋に入ってくると、若手スタートアップの人々が彼らを崇拝し、追い払うことがある。排他的ではなく、包括的であるべきだ。低心拍、欠乏マインドセットではなく、豊かさのマインドセットを持つ。

質問をするが、限度がある。質問は素晴らしいが、やりすぎると尋問のようになる。情報を抽出しようとし、何も与えないことがある。会話は与えることと得ることの交換であるべきだ。最良の会話は、アイデアやポイントを持って現れ、相手が反応し、双方向のやり取りがあるピンポンゲームのようなものだ。

語彙レベルを合わせる。大学教授と12歳に話す場合、彼らが使用する語彙レベルに合わせることで、リラックスして心地よく感じさせる。

もっと欲しいと思わせて終わる。会話の目的は、相手に「とても興味深い人だった。また会いたい」と思わせることだ。「この人の人生の物語をすべて聞いた。もう話す必要はない」と思わせてはいけない。適切に会話を終了させることも重要である。

有名人への接し方

有名人に対して、ファン狂いにならない。Mark Zuckerbergに近づき、「こんにちは、私はSamです。あなたは誰ですか」と言うのは馬鹿げている。逆に「会えて最も光栄です」と言うのも間違っている。彼らは人間であることを認識する。「これは私がMarkに会う唯一の機会ではない」と思い、会った後、「とても素敵な人だった。また話したい」と思わせることが最善だ。「メールアドレスと電話番号をください」と言うのはやめるべきだ。

**「会えて嬉しい」と「お会いできて嬉しい」**の違い。「お会いできて嬉しい」と言って、相手が「5回会ったことがある」と思ったら、非常に恥ずかしい。「会えて嬉しい」なら、会ったことがあってもなくても機能する。

衛生と服装

香りは控えめに。香水やコロンを浴びるように塗るべきではないが、悪臭を放つべきでもない。香りは目立つべきではない。性別に関係なく、香りは誰にとっても記憶に残るべきではない。

服装はワンレベル上に。2レベルや3レベルではなく、1レベル上にする。スーツを着ている場合、ジャケットを脱けば少しカジュアルになる。

フィット感がすべて。$20のよくフィットするシャツは、$500のフィットしていないシャツよりはるかに良い。スタートアップ創業者としてロレックスを着るのは、心拍が高すぎ、努力しすぎていることを示し、無教養に見える。

服装レベルがわからない場合は聞く。フォークの使い方や期待されることについて聞くことは完全に問題ない。自信、落ち着き、謙虚さを示す。

食事のエチケット

チップを十分に払う。**20%**は最低限。30%以上は少し馬鹿げているかもしれないが、チップが記憶に残るべきではない。会食の相手が1ヶ月後に夕食について振り返ったとき、会話の内容やビジネスの機会について考え、「あの人は信じられないほど多くチップを払った」とは思わせたくない。

メニューで最も高いものを注文しない。投資家は実際には気にしないかもしれないが、コストに対して不感覚な人であることに気づく。

最初に注文しない。誰かがトーンを設定するのを見て、そのトーンに合わせるのが良い。真ん中から最後あたりに行くのが良い。

支払いを申し出る。合理的な範囲で常に支払いを申し出る。VCと外食する場合、カードを出すと99%の時間は「私が払う」と言われる。$10,000の夕食や超級高級なワインを注文された場合を除き、支払いを申し出るのが丁寧なアプローチである。

B&Dトリック

左手のBはBread（パン）、右手のDはDrink（ドリンク）。大きなテーブルに座ったとき、どれが自分のものかわからなくなることがある。BとDは便利である。

ナプキンは膝に置く。首には置かない。ナプキンの配置は記憶に残るべきではない。

ユーモアと自己卑下

ユーモアは素晴らしいが、やりすぎるべきではない。部屋の雰囲気に条件付きで微妙である。汚いジョークを間違った部屋で言うべきではない。ジョークを語ることは、その空間での究極の快適さのデモンストレーションである。誰も笑わないジョークは大きなリスクである。

自分をからかう。他の人をからかうことは、非常に高いレベルの親密さを示す。自分をからかうことは常に楽しい。

ジョークのリストを持つ。EvernoteやBearアプリに、最も攻撃的でないものから最も攻撃的なものまでランク付けされたジョークのリストを持つ。

人を喜ばせる簡単な物語を持つ。10分間の物語ではなく、会話を運ぶ短い物語をいくつか持つ。

会話の終了

会話が終わったら、優雅に去る。最悪は、会話が終わったのにそこに立っていること。「飲み物を取りに行く」と言ったとき、それは「私も行きたい」という招待ではないことが多い。

他の人を会話に招き入れる。「この会話を楽しんだ。Steveに会ったことがあるか？Steveに会いに行こう」と言い、会話を次の人に渡す。

スケジューリングのエチケット

Calendlyをデフォルトにしない。権力階層や忙しさの階層を認識する。シニアでない人、忙しくない人は、相手がいつ空いているかを尋ね、自分のスケジュールで調整すべきである。最初の3つのうちの1つが機能すべきである。

再スケジュールする場合、可能な限り通知し、相手の都合に合わせる。

タイムゾーンを確認し、合理的な時間にスケジュールする。

EAやPAを尊重する。クラスレスに見える最善の方法は、他の人を助けている人を尊重しないことである。過剰にする必要はないが、深い敬意を持つべきである。スケジュールしてくれたときにありがとうと言い、フォローアップする。

通信とメールのエチケット

絵文字を使わない。ビジネス文脈では、絵文字は親密さの一歩であり、自分のリスクで使用すべきである。

校正する。要点に早く到達する。相手が忙しいと仮定する。

メールにはSLAで応答する。12ページのエッセイを返す必要はないが、少なくとも迅速な確認は必要である。

メールの送信順序を考える。最初の人がアシスタントで4番目の人がCEOなら、間違っている可能性がある。誰に送信しているか、誰をCCに入れているかには言語がある。CCラインは「コピーを持つべきだ」という意味であり、即時の応答は期待されていない。

BCCの場合、全員に返信しない。メールにいない人について話しているとき、その人をToフィールドに入れてスペルを確認して送信するのは最悪のエチケット違反である。

ミーティングのエチケット

10〜15分早く到着する。1時間早すぎるなら、ブロックを一周する。誰かのオフィスに座っていると、長く待っていたように感じる。

少しだけ小話から始める。天気や週末の話など、空気を少し切り、ビジネスに切り替えるのは良いことである。これは「私はビジネスのためにここにいるが、普通の人間であり、普通の会話をすることを知っている」というシグナルである。

仮想ミーティングではカメラをオンにする。ビデオ通話に適した服装と背景を持つ。背景にベッドがある場合、きれいに整えられているべきである。クローゼットを閉じる。

自分の後片付けをする。コーヒーカップをキッチンに置く場所を尋ねるか、サイドテーブルに置く。これは最悪の印象を与える簡単な方法を避ける。

退出とフォローアップ

人々がテーブルを離れるときに立つ。座ったまま握手をしない。

感謝でフォローアップする。短い感謝のノートを送る。会って何かを得たことを示す。「時間を取ってくれてありがとう」という言葉は常に感謝される。

電話に出る場合、「すぐに折り返す」と言ってから去る。そのまま電話に出て手を振らない。

大人数の設定ではアイルランド式の別れが最善。ホストに一人だけありがとうと言い、ただ消える。「みんなにさよならと言ってハグする」のは多すぎる。

記事のポイント

  • 低心拍の技術: エチケットの目的は、低い心拍で部屋に現れ、信頼を構築することである。
  • 豊かさのマインドセット: これは唯一のチャンスではなく、他の機会があると考え、自信と落ち着きを持って現れる。
  • 具体的なエチケット: 早めに到着、強い握手、名前を繰り返す、アイコンタクト、B&Dトリック、20%以上のチップ、支払いを申し出る。
  • スケジューリングと通信: Calendlyをデフォルトにしない、EAを尊重する、絵文字を使わない、メールの順序を考える。
  • 会話と関係構築: 質問はバランスよく、語彙レベルを合わせ、もっと欲しいと思わせて終わる、自分をからかう。

メタデータ

企業・組織: Slow Ventures, Facebook, Kleiner Perkins, Y Combinator, Bain & Company, Evernote, Calendly, Morning Brew, Cursor, DigitalOcean, Cloudflare, SpaceX, WorkOS, 10Web, DX
人物: Sam Lessin, Lenny Rachitsky, Gary Tan, Mark Zuckerberg, Marc Andreessen, Dave Morin, Garry Tan, Alison Gopnik
キーワード: エチケット, 信頼構築, 低心拍, スタートアップ, ビジネスマナー, 会話術, スケジューリング, 食事マナー, チップ, 自己プレゼンテーション
ワンライナー: 創業者のための信頼構築エチケット

7:10>> yeah this is the thing I think about a

7:14and I are now old people, right? But

7:16like,

7:17>> yes,

7:17>> you're young.

7:18>> I feel that.

7:18>> You you feel it in your bones. It's like

7:20you're young, right? You're in you're at

7:23the Kleiner Perkins holiday party. You

7:26have all the venture capital in the

7:27world and all the CEOs. You're at your

7:29first company. You're young. Maybe

7:30you're from a different country. You're

7:32like, "Oh my god, like this is my shot,

7:35right? Like I have all these people I

7:36need to talk to and I need to convince

7:37this person of that and make this

7:40transactional. If you show up like a

7:43little Energizer Bunny, right, you're

7:45gonna scare off like you're gonna

7:47project totally the wrong vibe. And I

7:49but I can understand why you'd be like a

7:50high intensity moment in a lot of ways.

7:53I think understanding how to show up,

7:55take a beat, come in with a a mindset

7:57not of scarcity but of abundance.

7:59Understand how to give more than you

8:01take. Understand how to build a

8:03relationship, not collect business

8:04cards. Like these are things that

8:06actually serve you massively well. And I

8:08just feel like no one's being honest in

8:09teaching founders this, right? Instead,

8:11they're saying, "Oh, no. All that

8:12matters is your product." I'm like,

8:14"Yeah, like the product does matter a

8:19can be the difference between doing

8:20really well and missing a business deal,

8:22you know, if you show up with too high a

8:23heart rate and you burn a bunch of mild

8:26relationship opportunities, like I don't

8:28know why you wouldn't want these skills,

8:29right?"

10:13>> Yeah, be early. That's the first one,

10:17right? Like, you know, again, I say this

10:19as someone who I'll be honest again,

10:21like hypocrisy, you know, I I live with

10:22hypocrisy. I'm frequently not early,

10:25right? But you should be early, right?

10:27Um, and you don't need to be half an

10:28hour early. That's a little weird. But,

10:30you know, making sure that you have some

10:32buffer time so that again, think about

10:34low heart rate. If you come in racing in

10:36the room five minutes late, your heart

10:37rate's up. If you come in, you had a

10:39second to beat in to take a beat in the

10:41in the waiting room. They kept you

10:42waiting, right? That that's the dynamic

10:44I think you want to to cultivate. Now,

10:46if you're not late, I mean, sorry, if

10:48you're not early, just apologize. It

10:50doesn't need to be like a five alarm

10:52fire. Again, it goes back to this heart

10:53rate thing. You could just apologize

10:55simply and move on. I've seen people

10:56screw this up so many times, right?

10:58Where they come in flustered and all

10:59over the place. You're like, "It's okay.

11:01We understand, right?" Like, uh, so I

11:03think that's kind of a another really

11:04kind of obvious one, but an important

11:06one. Something I've seen, I'll go for a

11:08few others that we talk a bunch about in

11:10kind of is look um you want to have a

11:13strong handshake, firm, don't crush the

11:15person's hand. Again, this is not, you

11:17know, practice on your friends, right?

11:19you know, you want to repeat names back

11:22is a really really valuable thing to

11:23think about when you're meeting someone.

11:25Say, "Hey, Lenny, it's great to meet

11:26you." Right? Why? It shows that like

11:28you're actually trying to remember the

11:30person's name, right? Like a lot of

11:31times people meet a lot of people. It's

11:32like, "Nice to meet you." You move on.

11:34First, it's going to be harder for you

11:35to remember the person's name. Second,

11:37it actually shows like you're meeting

11:38them and making an effort, right, to

11:41actually connect and say, "Okay, I'm

11:42trying to focus on you. You're not just

11:43a number to me, right? You're not just a

11:45potential check for whatever it's going

11:47to be." So, there's a bunch of things

11:48like that. I'm kind of curious, you

11:50know, we can go through a bunch more,

11:51but those are some of the ones I would

11:52think about.

11:52>> One that I loved was if somebody else is

11:54late, do not make them feel bad. Do the

11:57opposite of what you're doing of what

11:58you do.

12:01this with entrepreneurs and like I get

12:02like I'm a VC. I do get scheduled in

12:06like 30 minute chunks back toback like

12:08all the time, especially on Zoom. Guess

12:12good about it, but it happens, right?

12:18I'm late, I will always apologize. I'll

12:53contact. Share that one.

12:54>> It's really important. Again, I think

12:56the thing to keep in mind is especially

12:58in an age where everyone's used to being

13:00in front of computer screens and looking

13:02at six different windows at the same

13:04time. Again, people are taking their

13:06time to meet with you or at a party.

13:07They're like taking their time to like

13:09listen to you. And it's just a matter of

13:11respect to be like, I'm actually here to

13:14this conversation. I'm not off on my

13:15screen. I'm not like glancing around the

16:24Give us some tips.

16:25>> So, I mean on the conversations fronts,

16:27I mean I think the key again is to

16:29welcome people into the conversation.

16:31Like consider it, you know, there's

16:32especially you see this happen

16:34sometimes, especially when there's weird

16:36power dynamics at play. Um, you know,

16:38you'll see some famous VC or founder

16:41walk into the room and then some young

16:43startup person will like way them,

16:45right? And like kind of almost like

16:47flock them off, right? and they're like

16:48really excited to talk to this person,

16:50but you're like there's a bunch of

16:51people around and like the more you can

16:52be inclusive again, low heart rate,

16:54you're not like it's not a scarcity

16:56mindset, it's an abundance mindset. Like

16:58I think that's kind of like the the tone

16:59to think about in terms of like what a

17:02conversation is and like how to how to

17:04show up in a room and and meet with

17:05people. Um, you know, another really big

17:07one we we harp on a lot in the book in

17:09in kind of a bunch of the panels is like

17:11ask questions, but there's a limit,

17:15right? So asking questions is great.

17:16you're coming in, it says, "Hey, hey,

17:18it's nice to meet you. Let me give you

17:20my fourminute startup spiel and

17:21everything I'm into D is like so

17:23self-centered. It kind of misses the

17:25point that a conversation is a is a give

17:27and a get and it should be an exchange."

17:29And so when you go in with a mindset of

17:30I should ask questions, that's great.

17:32There is doing it too much, right? Which

17:35is when it's done in a forced way.

17:38Sometimes I feel this. you'll meet

17:39someone and you feel like it's the

17:41inquisition or like all they're trying

17:43to do is extract information to you and

17:44giving you nothing in return, right?

17:46Like this happens sometimes. And so I

17:48think like again this is about balance.

17:50This is about low heart rate. I do think

17:52questions are a great tool to engage

17:54someone. But don't make it six questions

17:56in a row and make sure there's always in

17:58some ways a give to get right. Like if

18:00you can come in the best conversations

18:02are coming in, someone comes in and

18:04gives you an idea or has a point or

18:06sparks something then you have it's like

18:07a game of ping pong like then you can

18:09kind of react to and it goes back and

18:11forth where there's openness and they're

18:13playing with you not playing single

18:15player right is almost the way I would

18:16think about it in a conversation.

18:17>> So the tip here so it's basically index

18:19towards asking questions but not like

18:20100%.

18:21>> Yeah. Consider almost put it this way.

18:23Imagine you're playing pingpong, right?

18:25Or tennis or whatever you want. Like hit

18:27the ball back, right? That's the

18:29question, right? And they'll hit it back

18:30to you and then you hit it back to them,

18:32right? Like that's kind of what the flow

18:33should be. If you hit 10 balls at them

18:35in a row or like you know what I mean?

18:37Or like you know that that's kind of

18:39like not the vibe you want to go for.

18:41>> Yep. Awesome. Look, we talk a lot about

18:43matching matching vocabulary. You're

18:45going to meet a lot of different people.

18:47you know, you want to make people feel

18:49good and welcome. You know, I'm not

18:52saying that, you know, you should walk

18:53into a room and start talking in J, but

18:55like I am saying like if you're speaking

18:58to a university professor versus a

18:5912-year-old, if they're using a certain

19:01level of vocabulary words, like again,

19:03the point is to meet people where

19:05they're at in a way that makes them feel

19:08relaxed and good, not try to like mirror

19:12them, if that makes sense. But there is

19:13a subtlety that I think really matters

19:15to it.

19:16>> Cool. There's a few more I'll point out

19:17real quick. Um, connecting to this idea

19:20of asking questions, not trying to give

19:21your whole spiel constantly. This idea

19:23of leaving them wanting more.

19:25>> Yes, this is important. I mean, I think

19:28in the end of the day, like most

19:29interactions, let's you meet someone

19:30you're really interested or whatever. If

19:33we're being transactional about it,

19:36what's the real goal? The real goal is

19:37to leave people in a position where

19:39they're like, "Wow, that was a really

19:40interesting person. I'd love to hear

19:41from them again or meet with them

19:42again." Or maybe even better, every once

19:44in a while this will happen. like, "Wow,

19:46that's a really interesting person or

19:48idea." The person who walks across the

19:50room say, "Hey, you really should talk

19:51to Lenny. Talk to Sam." Like, that's

19:53kind of like you want to leave them

19:55being like, "That's interesting. I like

19:56to continue this or expand it." Not I

19:59just heard this entire person's life

20:01story. I never need to talk to them

20:03again, right? And so, I do think

20:04there's, you know, again, like leave

20:05them wanting more, right? I think is

20:06important. And that that is partially

20:08about knowing when to excuse yourself

20:09gracefully as much as it is about when

20:11to enter.

21:13this is part of the story. This is not

22:34>> Well, again, Lenny, you and I probably

22:35use this all the time, I bet. Because I

22:37honestly, again, we go by name, face,

22:39whatever. It's really difficult social

22:41situation to put someone in it. Just

22:45you go up to someone and say, "Hey, it's

22:48really great. It's great to meet you."

22:53for them, for you, for everyone. the

27:59with many of them, and it's not their

28:06for months or whatever. And so like when

28:08they show up at your office to pitch you

28:11and you know they get a coffee or

28:13something and then they like leave it on

28:15the table and don't ask you where to put

28:16it. It's a subtle sign of like not being

28:19aware of your broader environment that

28:21you may or may not know. But like I

28:23think it's valuable. Maybe they're like

28:25this is the wrong thing to focus on. I

28:30>> I think the hygiene stuff I mean you

28:32should get the book. I think it's like

28:33fairly obvious hygiene stuff. like don't

28:35be covered in schmutz, you know what I

28:37mean? Like show respect. Try to

28:40anticipate how the room is going to be

28:42dressed and like don't massively

28:44overdress or underdress. It's like if

28:46you show up to a business casual thing

28:47in a tuxedo,

28:49you know, you're you're like you're kind

28:51of trying to stand out. Don't be

28:54also don't want to be memorable in the

29:18wasn't a maximum dress code. So, I

34:50someone takes you out to dinner like

34:52it's a VC another found like someone

34:54invites you

34:54>> partner or whatever whatever it is.

34:56Yeah. And look, I I think I always say

34:58in terms of this, which I obviously

34:59think is important, is like look,

35:01within reason,

35:03always offer to pay. Now, you should be

35:07turned down, right? If you go out with a

35:09VC, right, and you're like,

35:12you know, you put a card down,

35:1599% of the time, they be like, I got

35:16this. Like, please, like, this is don't

35:18worry about it, right? And like that's

35:20the right vibe on it. You do not need to

35:21do this if it's a $10,000 dinner. or if

35:23they've ordered a super expensive bottle

35:24of wine, like there there are limits to

35:26this, right? But if you go out to a

35:28normal dinner in a normal situation, you

35:30don't offer to split it. You just offer

35:32to pay for it, right? And then, you

35:34know, you should be declined on that.

35:36But there is a little bit of a risk

35:38there because someone might not decline

35:39you and then you kind of are on the hook

35:40for it. But that is, I think, the the

35:42polite thing and the polite way to

35:43approach it.

42:16next to you, but like it was really

43:10>> Here's the thing. Humor is great. I love

43:13humor. It can be overdone. And again, it

43:16shouldn't be the point of things. And

43:17also, I'd say humor is quite conditional

43:19and subtle to the to the audience. So,

43:21like in the the room you're in, dirty

43:24jokes, right? Do you tell a dirty you

43:27don't want to tell a dirty joke in the

43:28wrong room. uh you but I think the thing

43:30the thing about humor is it kind of

43:32there's this interesting subtlety to why

43:34it's so useful in social studies which

43:36is one it kind of shows you the ultimate

43:38mastery of a social situation if you're

43:40able to tell a joke which is right up

43:42the line or even pushes it one degree to

43:45show your own comfort in the space right

43:47it's the ultimate demonstration of

43:48comfort in a space is to tell a joke

43:51that's a little over the line or a

43:53little off color but not too off color

43:58you're really in it and like feeling

44:00good using humor is great. You should

44:02not be remembered as only the comedian.

44:07you're playing with is like a very

44:08subtle thing, right? So, you don't want

44:10to tell a knockk knockock joke, right?

44:13When like with adults, right? But the

44:16off-color sex joke that is hilarious,

44:24the last thing you want, like a joke

44:26that everyone laughs at is great. a joke

47:08minutes, right? But again, I think the

47:11thing about is like imagine that the

47:12whole game in these social settings

47:14again is putting other people at ease,

47:16making them feel like you understand

47:18them and the room and you're a

47:19trustworthy person and on the same

47:21wavelength. And in some ways, think

47:29where questions come in and back and

47:30forth having a fun build on story. They

47:37a conversation or these social

47:38interactions is a pingpong game, right?

47:42of those in your arsenal.

47:43>> Your last tip in that section was how to

47:45wind down a conversation. Just the

47:46importance of realizing it's time to

47:47wind down. Any tips for how to do that?

47:49>> Yeah, just like when the conversation is

47:50over, gracefully leave basically the

47:53upshot. You people the worst is like the

47:55conversation ends and the person just

47:57stands there and you're like, uh, I'm

47:59going to go get a drink, you know, or do

48:01you want And then every once in a while

48:02the person will be like you're like if

48:04someone says to you in a conversation

48:05they wind it down. It's like I'm going

48:06to go grab a drink.

48:09Most of the time that is not an

48:11invitation to say like I'd love one too

48:13and follow them to the bar, right? Like

48:14that that is not. So like I just think

48:16you have to kind of recognize the signs

48:18effectively when the moment is passing

48:21or it's time to move on etc. Um and then

48:24like respect it. Uh that's what I would

48:26say.

48:26>> I I use that one all the time. Is there

48:28is there any other ways you find useful

48:30to wind down a conversation? just going

48:31to get out of a conversation.

48:32>> Well, I I think I think the other thing

48:34people do frequently, which again is

48:35totally fine if done respectfully, is

48:37bring someone else into the

48:38conversation, right? Like in some ways

48:40like give them their next partner, you

48:41know, be like, "Oh, like I've enjoyed

48:43this conversation. It's super cool. You

48:46know, you know, by the way, have you met

48:47Steve? Let's go meet Steve. Go talk to

48:50Steve for a bit." Right? And like I'll

48:52pick you back up. I'm gonna Okay, I'm

48:53going to go say hi to my wife. You know,

48:54like there there's ways to handle it

48:56again. Like the key though is subtlety

48:58on these things, right? Like I think in

49:00all things like you want to basically

49:01let people feel respected as much as

49:04anything else. So if you if you're too

49:06overt about it, right? Um you don't give

49:09them even if everyone kind of knows

49:11what's going on, the key is to give them

49:13like plausible deniability to themselves

49:15and the community effectively that like

49:18we've wound down this conversation. So

49:23sections. Uh the next one is scheduling

49:25etiquette. Give us some advice. Oh,

49:26well, like I think I'd like to say that

49:28I'm fairly famous for hating Calendarly.

49:30Um, there was a I actually I I think I'm

49:33not overstating it when saying there was

49:35a period where Calendarly where I

49:36personally had driven most of their

49:37growth for the month. I got messages

49:39from the board because I went on this

49:40diet tribe about how much I hate Countly

49:43and how disrespectful it is. And

49:45apparently this was such an internet

49:47fervor. I got like millions of

49:48engagements that meaningfully drove

49:50their month. Um, thank no bad press.

49:54>> No bad press. No bad tweets. Yeah. So, I

49:56think like look, there's a few obvious

49:58ones. When you're make sure you have

50:00availability, you know, like if you ask

50:01someone to schedule with you, it's not

50:02always wrong to be like here's my

50:04availability or here's a link, but like

50:07make it real. Like you have to give them

50:08real options. You know, the default I

50:11really strongly believe the default

50:12should not be calendarly. The default in

50:14most situations, especially if you

50:16recognize the power hierarchy, the

50:18busyiness hierarchy. If you're the less

50:20senior person, if you are the less busy

50:22person, you should let the other person

50:25tell you when they're free and then make

50:27it work on your end, right? Uh is what I

50:29would say. And it's fine if the first

50:30thought doesn't work, but the one of the

50:32first three really needs to, right? So,

50:33I think that like in some ways that it's

50:36important to kind of respect that it's

50:37better to ask what they can do and then

50:39move your schedule. If you really can't

50:41and you're going to use a scheduling

50:42agent or something, it just needs to

50:44actually have real options is basically

50:46I think a thing that comes up all the

50:47time. Um, look, rescheduling happens.

50:52When you do it, give notice as much as

50:54possible. Uh, once you're asking for

50:57rescheduling, you need to be even more

50:59accepting of what the other person can

51:00do, right? I think is really important.

51:02Like if you're asking to reschedule, you

51:04basically within reason need to make it

51:06work for them, right? Uh, is what I

51:08would say. And then look, I I think like

51:10there's like obvious stuff that people

51:11should know but sometimes forget. Like

51:13time zones are really tricky. People

51:15screw them up all the time. Check. It's

51:17it's worth the extra check to make sure

51:18you're not both scheduling uh you're

51:21getting the numbers right but then also

51:22really importantly scheduling at like

51:24reasonable times like sometimes people

51:25like I want to meet at this time you're

51:26like you're on EST you're that's like 4

51:30in the morning for me right and so like

51:31I think being respectful of that and

51:32just asking I think is super important

51:34it's not rocket science it's important

51:36last point I make which you make in the

51:37book is like you really need to respect

51:41EAS and PAS and the whole people like

51:44this is the the number one look way to

51:46look extreme extremely classless is to

51:48not respect people who are helping the

51:51other person, right? U like this is like

51:53the number one thing. Now, you don't

51:54need to be so overthe-top ex like you

51:57don't want to overdo it, but there

51:59should be this deep well of like respect

52:01for anyone who's helping you, whether

52:03that's a server or a PA or an EA or

52:05whatever there. This needs to come with

52:07an extra gesture of respect. That means

52:10saying thank you when they schedule and

52:12like following up with them and things

52:13like that. There's a I'll tell a story.

52:15When we were selling our company to

52:17Airbnb, we had this guy helping us sell

52:20the company and he made it a big point

52:22to build a good relationship with the EA

52:24and office manager at Airbnb because

52:27they if they like you, they'll it helps

52:30it helps you get the thing.

52:31>> Make the general story of like make the

52:33gatekeepers happy and like you is true,

52:35but I I I do think there ways to overdo

52:37that for what it's worth. It becomes

52:38almost too transactional. if you show up

52:40with flowers for the EA, you know, like

52:44you better be damn confident like in

52:45what you're doing, if that makes sense.

52:47But yeah, um just like the small things

52:49go a long way. Like you just, you know,

52:55if if they bring you a coffee cup, ask

52:56them where to put it when you're done.

55:25topic. Communication. Don't use emojis.

55:28>> Try to proofread your stuff. Get to the

55:31point quickly. Assume the person you're

55:32reading is busy. Like, again, I I think

55:34these are all the types of things that

55:36like none of this is rocket science from

55:38my perspective. But people, it's just

55:45people have different things on this. I

55:46personally do think that on things like

55:50email, you kind of do have an SLA to

55:53respond. There's some people I know who

55:54are like, "Email does not mean I have to

55:56respond. You send me an email, you may

55:58or may not get a response. It's

55:59completely up to me. I have no contract

56:00to respond to your email." I I

56:02personally go the other way, which is I

56:04feel like from an etiquette perspective,

56:06like I don't owe you a 12-page essay,

56:09but I do owe at least an acknowledgement

56:11quickly, right, of what you've sent?

56:13Like you don't want to leave people

56:14hanging. But again, I don't I just think

56:16it's like if you read an email, I'm

56:19sorry, if you write an email, imagine

56:20you're receiving it. How does it feel?

56:23Right? Does it feel like you're asking a

56:25ton of the person you sent it to? If you

56:26send them 10 paragraphs, it's annoying,

56:29right? You're like, "Okay, I have to

56:30read all this. What am I going to find

56:32time? Like this is like you're asking a

56:34lot. It's kind of like a monologue in a

56:36conversation. You've just said I'm going

56:37to spend 10 minutes talking at you,

56:39right? And so I do think keeping it

56:40short and to the point, you know, not

56:43being silly, not using emoji, trying to

56:45make it readable, like these are all

56:47important things.

57:25>> look, I think text is different. I think

59:05is uh think about the order of the

59:06emails when you're emailing somebody

59:09>> yeah I I do think you kind of want to

59:11think about you There is a connotation

59:14to who you send it to and who you CC and

59:17the order in which people know now I you

59:19know I don't want to like overstate this

59:21but put differently it's like if you're

59:23sending an email and the first person on

59:24the email is the assistant and the

59:28you've probably done it wrong

59:30>> and because the implication there is you

59:32who you think is most important comes

59:33first like who's first to put to mind

59:35>> who are you really sending this to you

59:37know what I mean is almost the way I

59:38think and like who's kind of included so

59:39like if I look at an email and I'm the

59:42first person in the two, right?

59:45Candidly, I I mildly pay more attention

59:47to it than when I'm the fifth, right?

59:48Because in my head, I'm like, "Okay,

59:50well, like

59:51>> this is really to Kevin and like I'm on

59:53it." Like if you see an email sent to

59:54many many many people, almost by

59:57definition, it's not that important,

59:58right? Is almost the way I would put it.

60:00And so, you have to be really careful

60:01with manage that. I think even the the

60:02who do you send it to and who do you CC,

60:05there's a language to that that like

60:07from an etiquette perspective to

60:08understand, right? And like I I do think

60:10people sometimes miss that. Like the CC

60:12line is very very valuable. It means,

60:14hey, you should have a copy of this.

60:16This is not really to you. I'm not

60:17expecting an immediate response, right?

60:19I even think there's even a subtlety

60:20like who responds then. Like if you send

60:22an email to 10 people CCD to the whole

60:25nine yards, you know, there is a subtle

60:27etiquette to like when you respond. Like

60:30if you are the fifth CC on an email,

60:34you're not expected to be the first

60:36response. Again, you can break this

60:38rule. Like, there are times to break it.

60:40There's a subtlety to it, but it wasn't

60:42really sent to you, right? Like, and so

60:44there's a whole language to who you send

60:45to and who you CC and the order that

60:47like again, it's very subtle, but is

60:50worth understanding.

60:51>> So true, man. And you would think nobody

62:32etiquette.

62:33>> Yeah. I mean, again, we've talked about

62:34arriving a little bit early. You should

62:36do that. Don't arrive up too too early.

62:39Like, you shouldn't again like if you're

62:40an hour early, walk around the block.

62:43It's fine to walk around the block. You

62:45don't want to sit in someone's office

62:46because then all of a sudden it feels

62:47like this person has been here a long

62:48time. Like this feels like even though

62:50they're not scheduled, like we're

62:51leaving them hanging. You're six coffees

62:53deep with the receptionist. Like you

62:55don't want that, right? Like uh so I

62:58think you want to be like 10 to 15

62:59minutes early. You do not want to be

63:00much earlier than that. Um we talked

63:03about meeting other convenience. I do

63:05think it's fine to start with a little

63:07bit of small talk, right? uh you know

63:10there are times it's not or times people

63:11running behind but like the pleasantry

63:13of like the weather is nice or how was

63:17your weekend or like something that kind

63:19of cuts cuts the air a little bit and

64:02>> so that you're ready to talk so that

64:04Yeah.

64:04>> Yeah. um for meetings, virtual ones,

64:08camera on. Like, and again, I say this

64:11as someone who sometimes violates this,

64:13right? I violate it knowingly. I violate

64:15it knowing what it costs me, but you

64:17really should have your camera on,

64:19right? Um and again, like you should

64:21dress appropriately for a video call.

64:22You should have an appropriate

64:23background. You know, if you have your

64:25bed in your background, it should be

64:27nicely made. You know what I mean? Like,

64:29in some ways, it's like doing the easy

64:30stuff is the key in some way. And I I go

64:33a step further. I actually, this is this

64:35is less a hard rule. I actually really

64:39don't love virtual backgrounds for the

64:41same reason. I'm like, look, I'm not

64:43going to judge you if you're in your

64:45bedroom, if you're a startup founder.

64:46It's fine, but I would like to see that

64:50your bed is made or like I'll give you

64:52another one that's classic that I see

64:53with founders all the time. Close your

64:56closet. Like people will get on Zoom

64:58calls and you'll be on a call and like

65:00their closets open. And I'm like it's

65:02not a big deal, but like do you see your

65:06own self picture here? Like can you just

65:08close your closet? I don't want to see

65:10your shirts, you know? Like that that

65:12type of stuff I think goes further than

65:14people realize.

65:16>> Awesome. Uh one other tip you had was

65:18clean up after yourself if you're in a

65:19real meeting. This is actually the

65:21easiest way

65:22>> and this, by the way, is for my partner

65:23Kevin Collard, but the easiest way to

65:27come off badly is to not offer to put

65:31your coffee cup in the kitchen, right?

65:33And like honestly, we do this like we

65:35because like if you think about it, we

65:36work for LPs, limited partners, right?

65:38Like that's who we raise money from and

65:39then deploy it from. And my partner

65:41Kevin, even more than me, has this

65:43thing, which is he is maniacal about

65:45this, which is no matter who's in the

65:46room, if we're with an LP, you take the

65:48coffee, you take the diet coke, even if

65:50you know full well someone's going to

65:51come in and clean it back for you, and

65:53you make a point of asking where we can

65:55put it or putting on the side table,

65:57etc., and acknowledging that there is a

65:58mess. I feel that. Final topic is

66:02exiting and leaving. What are some tips?

66:05>> You should stand when people leave the

66:06table, right? Not ridiculously, by the

66:08way. you should go stand with the table.

66:10Stand to shake hands. Don't be sitting

66:12when you're shaking hands. It's just

66:13what you do, right? And like it shows

66:15that you're aware of it. Um follow up

66:17with gratitude. Like you should send

66:20people thank you notes. Even if they

66:21shouldn't be long, they shouldn't be

66:23ridiculous, but like we met, I got

66:26something out of it. Like thank you for

66:28taking your time is like always

66:30appreciated. Uh is what I would say. Um

66:33obviously there's stuff like don't take

66:34calls, etc. Like this is kind of like

66:36the kind of obvious stuff in terms of

66:38exiting and how you think about it. Like

66:40even if someone if someone rings you,

66:41right, and you're like and the meeting's

66:44over and you're over time, you're like,

66:45I got to pick up this call. You hit the

66:47button says, I'm calling you right back

66:48and then walk away. Don't just pick up

66:50the phone and like wave, you know, is

66:52what I would say. Um yeah, and I I think

66:54like there's stuff like that um that I

66:56would just keep in mind.

66:57>> They also had like don't make a

66:58production if you're exiting, just like

83:42like it's kind of a weird podcast in

83:44some ways because honestly, I don't

83:46think any of us are highly filtered and

83:49it's kind of just like talking to your

83:50friends for an hour a week, which I want

83:52to do. Like, I love the world. We don't

83:53live that close to them. So, it's kind

83:54of fun to just like have that time set

83:56aside and then cut it up and post on the

83:58internet pretty raw. Um, here's the

84:00funny thing about it. It's fine. It

84:03serves our purposes way more than I

84:07actually expected it to, which is we

84:09enjoy doing it, which is the most

84:11important part. We would do it if no one

84:12was listening. And weirdly, like a lot

84:22weird is like I don't like it's not a
